questions.question:
Discuss the five pillars of Islam.
(a) Name all five pillars.
(b) Explain any THREE in detail.
(c) State the importance of the five pillars to a Muslim.
questions.answer:
(a) The Five Pillars of Islam:
1. Shahadah (Declaration of Faith)
2. Salat (Prayer)
3. Zakat (Almsgiving)
4. Sawm (Fasting)
5. Hajj (Pilgrimage)

(b) Explanation of three pillars:

1. Shahadah (Declaration of Faith):
The Shahadah is the testimony: 'There is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is the messenger of Allah.' It is the foundation of all Islamic belief. Pronouncing the Shahadah with sincere conviction is the entry point into Islam. It affirms absolute monotheism (Tawhid) and the prophethood of Muhammad.

2. Salat (Prayer):
Muslims are obligated to pray five times daily — Fajr (dawn), Dhuhr (midday), Asr (afternoon), Maghrib (sunset), and Isha (night). Before prayer, ritual purification (Wudu) must be performed. Muslims face the Qibla (direction of the Kaabah in Mecca) during prayer. Salat maintains a continuous connection between the believer and Allah throughout the day.

3. Zakat (Almsgiving):
Zakat is the obligatory annual payment of 2.5% of a Muslim's accumulated savings and wealth (above the nisab threshold) to those in need. It purifies wealth, reduces inequality, and demonstrates solidarity with the poor. It is given to eight categories including the poor, the needy, debtors, and travellers in difficulty.

4. Sawm (Fasting in Ramadan):
During the holy month of Ramadan, Muslims fast from dawn (Fajr) to sunset (Maghrib), abstaining from food, drink, smoking, and marital relations. Fasting develops self-discipline, God-consciousness (Taqwa), gratitude, and empathy for the poor. The fast ends each day with Iftar (breaking of fast) and the month ends with Eid al-Fitr.

5. Hajj (Pilgrimage):
Hajj is the annual pilgrimage to Mecca, obligatory once in a lifetime for every Muslim who is physically and financially able. It takes place in the Islamic month of Dhul Hijja. Pilgrims perform rituals including Tawaf (circling the Kaabah), Sa'i (walking between Safa and Marwa), and standing on the plain of Arafat. Hajj is the largest annual gathering of people on earth.

(c) Importance of the five pillars:
1. They structure and regulate a Muslim's spiritual and daily life.
2. They unite Muslims globally regardless of race, nationality, or wealth.
3. They strengthen the individual's relationship with Allah.
4. They promote social cohesion, equality, and care for the less fortunate.

questions.question:
Explain the life and significance of Prophet Muhammad.
(a) Describe his early life and call to prophethood.
(b) Explain the significance of the Hijrah.
(c) State THREE contributions of Prophet Muhammad to humanity.
questions.answer:
(a) Early life and call to prophethood:
Prophet Muhammad ibn Abdullah was born in Mecca around 570 CE. He lost his father before birth and his mother Aminah when he was six years old. He was raised by his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib and later his uncle Abu Talib. He was known as Al-Amin (the trustworthy) for his honesty and integrity.

At age 25, he married the wealthy businesswoman Khadijah, who was 15 years his senior. He would often retreat to the Cave of Hira on Mount Nur for meditation and reflection.

At age 40 (610 CE), the angel Jibril (Gabriel) appeared to him in the cave and revealed the first words of the Quran: 'Read in the name of your Lord who created...' (Surah Al-Alaq 96:1). Initially frightened, Muhammad was reassured by Khadijah and began receiving revelations over the next 23 years. He began preaching the message of Tawhid (monotheism), calling people away from idol worship.

(b) Significance of the Hijrah (622 CE):
The Hijrah is Prophet Muhammad's migration with his followers from Mecca to Madinah (then Yathrib) in 622 CE, after years of persecution by Meccan leaders.

Significance:
1. It marks the beginning of the Islamic calendar (AH — After Hijrah).
2. It established the first Islamic state and community (Ummah) in Madinah.
3. It demonstrated the Islamic principle that believers should support one another.
4. In Madinah, the Prophet established the Constitution of Madinah — one of the earliest written constitutional agreements — providing rights for Muslims, Jews, and other communities.
5. It shows that believers may need to sacrifice comfort for the sake of their faith.

(c) Three contributions to humanity:
1. Spiritual guidance — He delivered the Quran, the final revelation of Allah, providing comprehensive guidance for all aspects of human life.
2. Social reform — He abolished tribal discrimination, elevated the status of women and the poor, abolished infanticide, and established rights for slaves.
3. Establishing justice and governance — In Madinah, he established a model of just governance, resolving disputes fairly and creating a multi-religious covenant of mutual rights.

questions.question:
Discuss the concept of Tawhid (Oneness of Allah) in Islam.
(a) Define Tawhid and state its three categories.
(b) Explain how Tawhid is expressed in Islamic worship.
(c) Explain the concept of Shirk and why it is considered the gravest sin in Islam.
questions.answer:
(a) Definition and three categories of Tawhid:
Tawhid is the central doctrine of Islam — the absolute oneness, uniqueness, and incomparability of Allah. It is the foundation upon which all of Islam is built.

Three categories:
1. Tawhid ar-Rububiyyah (Oneness of Lordship): Allah alone is the Creator, Sustainer, and Controller of the universe. He has no partners in His dominion.

2. Tawhid al-Uluhiyyah (Oneness of Worship): All worship — prayer, fasting, sacrifice, supplication — must be directed to Allah alone. No created being deserves worship.

3. Tawhid al-Asma was-Sifat (Oneness of Names and Attributes): Allah's names and attributes (such as the 99 Beautiful Names) are unique to Him and should neither be denied nor compared to creation.

(b) How Tawhid is expressed in Islamic worship:
1. The Shahadah explicitly declares monotheism: 'There is no god but Allah.'
2. All five daily prayers (Salat) are directed exclusively to Allah — no saint or intermediary is worshipped.
3. The Quran repeatedly emphasises that Allah has no son, no partner, and no equal (Surah Al-Ikhlas 112).
4. Dua (supplication) is made directly to Allah without intermediaries.
5. Tawaf (circling the Kaabah) is not worship of the Kaabah but of the Allah it symbolises as a direction of unity.

(c) Shirk and why it is the gravest sin:
Shirk means associating partners with Allah — attributing divine qualities, worship, or authority to anyone or anything other than Allah.

Forms of Shirk:
— Major Shirk: Worshipping idols, praying to saints or graves, attributing divine powers to humans.
— Minor Shirk: Showing off in worship (Riya), swearing by other than Allah.

Why it is the gravest sin:
1. The Quran states: 'Indeed, Allah does not forgive association with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills.' (Quran 4:48)
2. It is a direct negation of the entire message brought by all prophets from Adam to Muhammad.
3. It corrupts a person's relationship with Allah completely.
4. The Prophet Muhammad said the greatest sins are Shirk, disrespecting parents, and false testimony.

questions.question:
Discuss the spread of Islam in Nigeria.
(a) How and when did Islam arrive in Nigeria?
(b) Describe the Jihad of Usman Dan Fodio (1804).
(c) State THREE effects of the Dan Fodio Jihad on Northern Nigeria.
questions.answer:
(a) How Islam arrived in Nigeria:
Islam arrived in Nigeria gradually from the 11th century onwards, primarily through:
1. Trans-Saharan trade routes — Arab and Berber traders from North Africa brought Islam to the Kanem-Bornu Empire (around Lake Chad) as early as the 11th century.
2. Muslim scholars and teachers — Islamic clerics and learned men (Ulama) settled in Hausa states and taught the religion.
3. The Kanem-Bornu Empire — One of the earliest Islamic states in the region, whose rulers adopted Islam around the 11th century and encouraged its spread.
4. By the 14th-16th centuries, Islam had spread across the Hausa states of Kano, Katsina, Zaria, and others.

However, early Islam in the Hausa states was often mixed with traditional practices — rulers claimed to be Muslim but continued pagan customs.

(b) The Jihad of Usman Dan Fodio (1804):
Usman Dan Fodio (1754-1817) was a Fulani Islamic scholar and teacher in the Hausa state of Gobir. He was deeply concerned about the corruption of Islam in Hausaland — rulers were mixing Islamic practice with paganism, oppressing the poor, and taxing Muslims unjustly.

After years of preaching, conflict with the Sultan of Gobir escalated. In 1804, Dan Fodio launched a Jihad (holy struggle) for the purification of Islam and the establishment of an Islamic state. His forces defeated the Hausa rulers one by one.

By 1808, the Sokoto Caliphate was established — a federation of Emirates stretching across much of present-day northern Nigeria. Dan Fodio became Sultan and divided governance between his son Muhammad Bello (who succeeded him) and his brother Abdullahi.

(c) Three effects of the Dan Fodio Jihad:
1. Establishment of the Sokoto Caliphate — A large Islamic state that standardised Islamic governance, education, and law (Sharia) across northern Nigeria.
2. Expansion of Islamic education — Madrasahs (Islamic schools) were established, producing generations of Islamic scholars and administrators.
3. Social reforms — The Jihad aimed to protect the poor from oppressive taxation and establish justice; however, it also led to significant displacement of populations and enslavement of defeated peoples.
4. Political restructuring — The old Hausa ruling dynasties were replaced by Fulani Emirates, reshaping the political landscape of northern Nigeria to this day.

questions.question:
Discuss Islamic ethics and values.
(a) Explain FOUR core Islamic ethical values.
(b) Explain the Islamic concept of human rights.
(c) How can Islamic ethics guide Nigerian Muslims in contemporary society?
questions.answer:
(a) Four core Islamic ethical values:

1. Amanah (Trustworthiness):
Amanah means fulfilling one's responsibilities, keeping promises, and being reliable in all dealings. It applies to relationships with Allah (fulfilling worship obligations) and with fellow humans (honesty in business, family, and public life). The Prophet Muhammad was known as Al-Amin (the trustworthy) before his prophethood.

2. Adl (Justice):
Islam places great emphasis on justice — in personal conduct, business transactions, governance, and social relations. The Quran commands: 'O you who believe! Stand firmly for justice, as witnesses to Allah.' (4:135). Justice applies even to one's enemies.

3. Ihsan (Excellence and Benevolence):
Ihsan means worshipping Allah as if you see Him (knowing He sees you) and treating people with the highest standard of care and compassion. It goes beyond mere compliance to a standard of excellence in all actions.

4. Tawadu (Humility):
Islam condemns arrogance and pride, considering them dangerous spiritual diseases. Prophet Muhammad demonstrated extraordinary humility despite his status. Muslims are encouraged to be humble before Allah and respectful toward fellow human beings regardless of status.

(b) Islamic concept of human rights:
Islam established comprehensive human rights centuries before modern international human rights declarations. These include:
— Right to life: Life is sacred; murder is strictly forbidden.
— Right to dignity: Every human has inherent dignity regardless of race, gender, or wealth.
— Right to justice: Everyone deserves fair treatment before the law.
— Right to freedom of conscience and belief.
— Right to property: Wealth is protected; theft and fraud are forbidden.
— Rights of women: Women have rights to education, property, marriage consent, and inheritance.
— Rights of minorities: Non-Muslims under Islamic governance have protected rights (Dhimmi status).

(c) How Islamic ethics can guide Nigerian Muslims today:
1. Tackling corruption — The Islamic emphasis on Amanah and Adl can motivate Muslim officials to serve honestly and reject bribery and corruption.
2. Social responsibility — Zakat and Sadaqah principles encourage wealthy Muslims to invest in the welfare of the poor and underserved communities.
3. Conflict resolution — The Islamic values of forgiveness, justice, and dialogue can contribute to resolving ethnic and religious conflicts in Nigeria.
4. Environmental stewardship — The Islamic concept of Khalifa (stewardship of the earth) motivates responsible environmental practices.
